Well, so, from the technical side...
I am not sure if the sitemap is updating, so when I looked at it, there was only about 200-ish pages in there.
I definitely know there's more than that on the website, so that might be something to look into.
Google does look at the sitemap as a key indicator of what your top pages are for your site, so I think that's something that should be fixed right away.

There were duplicate H1s found on most of the landing pages, so it looks like in the hamburger menu when you click that, and open it, the log into my account text is actually being wrapped in an H1, and not ideally, it's the one that Google finds first.
So typically, when Google indexes pages, when it finds links or when it finds heading tags, it usually picks the first one it finds and sticks to that.
So all the H1s on all of your pages are bound to log into my account, so I would fix that right away as well.
There are a few pages that I found that have duplicate pages.
We found that the current way that the blog pages are set up, there is no category for each of the blog posts.
So there is a read more button on the blog page, which loads more blogs.
Typically, when I look at something like that, Google can't click that page.
So when you click on your load more, Google can't do that.
So all of those blogs that are below that load more section are orphaned.
So there's no actual physical link to those pages, except if you have like a article carousel.
So I would fix that right away.
An easy way to do that is to either just load all the blog posts in one shot, provide pagination.
So you can add pages and pagination to allow Google to crawl those pages, or you can create category pages.
With that route where we're creating category pages to load all the blogs within, which just gives them a little bit more topical relevance and defines your software a little bit better.
We found that the H2s and H3s were basically not, they weren't including any SEO keywords in them.
So one of them was just like learn more and another one was something else.
I can get into that a little bit later.
There were some signs that there were some client side JavaScript that was being rendered and Google can't crawl any of that content that's being rendered client side.
So we'd want to switch that over to server side so that Google can see that.
And then I did find that the ketamine page is showing different content to Google than it is to its users.
That is a concern to me because that's technically cloaking, especially when the content is drastically different.
And Google will penalize for that.

So if Google does find out that you are cloaking or if someone reports to you that you're cloaking, that could be a potential issue that causes that page not to rank or to hurt the positions of those pages.
Might just be part of the A-B test we're running for conversion rate optimization, though, not something like SEO manipulation.
Is there a different way to do that?

One is I call CCRR.
It's a cut, consolidate, refresh, and refactor content.

What content isn't performing?
Is it content that could potentially be hurting your site if it's not performing?
So it could be thin pages.
could be stuff that's not on brand or topic that potentially could be hurting your brand or hurting rankings, refreshing your top performing content.
So this is really just like building a moat, right?
So we want to make sure that your top performing content stays top performing.
So we really want to make sure we're building a moat there.
And then refactoring content, which is basically looking at, okay, this content is not ranking.
I think we have a right to rank in this topic or this space.
Like maybe it's a different angle.
Maybe you have to completely take a different view of this article.
It could be going from a human interest story to a listicle format, for example.
So just changing our take on what that particular article is so that we earn our right to win in that top 10 position.
